variables.dl: Infinitesimal length element along Amperian loop (m)
variables.I_enc: Total current enclosed by the Amperian loop (A)
variables.n: Number of turns per unit length of solenoid (turns/m)
variables.r: Radius of toroid or distance from axis (m)

locked_facts: The line integral is taken over a closed Amperian loop
Only the current passing THROUGH the enclosed area contributes to the integral
Magnetic field inside an ideal solenoid: B = μ₀nI (uniform, along axis)
Magnetic field outside an ideal solenoid: B = 0
Magnetic field inside a toroid: B = μ₀NI/(2πr)
Ampere's law is most useful when there is high symmetry (cylindrical, planar, toroidal)
truth_statements: Ampere's circuital law states that the line integral of the magnetic field around any closed loop equals μ₀ times the net current enclosed by that loop
For an ideal solenoid of n turns per unit length, the interior field is uniform at B = μ₀nI and the exterior field is zero
For a toroid with N total turns, the field inside the toroid at radius r is B = μ₀NI/(2πr) and zero outside
The choice of Amperian loop is guided by symmetry — the loop must be chosen so that B·dl simplifies to B × (length) on symmetric segments
Ampere's law is the magnetic analogue of Gauss's law — both exploit symmetry to calculate fields without full integration

aha_moment.what_to_show: A solenoid cross-section with an Amperian rectangle. The top side inside the solenoid has B·dl = B×L (field parallel to dl). The bottom side outside has B = 0. The two vertical sides contribute zero because B is perpendicular to dl. So ∮B·dl = BL = μ₀nIL, giving B = μ₀nI.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Highlight each side of the rectangular Amperian loop separately — show which contribute to the integral and which give zero. The non-obvious zero contributions (sides and outside) are the key insight.
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students assume the field leaks outside the solenoid or that all sides of the Amperian loop contribute equally — they see that clever loop choice makes the calculation tractable

simulation_states.label: Current-carrying solenoid
simulation_states.description: A solenoid shown with current direction marked on each turn. Field lines drawn inside (parallel, uniform) and outside (zero). No Amperian loop yet.
simulation_states.emphasis: Inside the solenoid: uniform field along the axis. Outside: field is negligibly small.

simulation_states.label: Amperian rectangle appears
simulation_states.description: A rectangular Amperian loop is drawn with one long side inside and one outside the solenoid. The four sides are colour-coded. Current threads through the rectangle are counted — nL turns enclosed.
simulation_states.emphasis: The Amperian loop encloses nL turns of current. We evaluate ∮B·dl along each side separately.

simulation_states.label: Evaluating the line integral
simulation_states.description: Each side lights up in sequence: (1) Inside top: B·dl = BL (parallel). (2) Outside bottom: B = 0, contribution = 0. (3) Left vertical: B ⊥ dl, contribution = 0. (4) Right vertical: B ⊥ dl, contribution = 0. Result: BL = μ₀nIL → B = μ₀nI.
simulation_states.emphasis: Only one side contributes! The clever rectangular loop makes three sides vanish. This is the power of Ampere's law with symmetry.

simulation_states.label: Interactive exploration
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts current I, turns density n, and can switch between solenoid and toroid geometries. Field magnitude updates in real time. For toroid, the radius r can be varied to see B = μ₀NI/(2πr).
simulation_states.emphasis: Compare solenoid (B = μ₀nI, uniform) with toroid (B = μ₀NI/(2πr), varies with r). Increase n or I to see B grow linearly.

common_misconceptions.belief: All currents near the Amperian loop contribute to ∮B·dl, not just the enclosed current
trigger_phrases: nearby current bhi count hota hai
common_misconceptions.correction: Only the current that actually passes THROUGH the surface bounded by the Amperian loop contributes to ∮B·dl. Currents outside the loop affect the local B field at each point but their contributions cancel when integrated around the full closed loop.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show an external wire near the loop — its field contributions on opposite sides of the loop cancel out in the line integral

common_misconceptions.belief: The magnetic field outside a solenoid is exactly zero everywhere
trigger_phrases: solenoid ke bahar koi field nahi
common_misconceptions.correction: For an IDEAL infinite solenoid, the field outside is zero. Real solenoids are finite, so there is a weak field outside, especially near the ends. The ideal result is an approximation that works well for long solenoids far from the ends.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show a finite solenoid with weak field lines curving outside near the ends, contrasted with the ideal infinite case

legend.meaning: Line integral of B around closed Amperian loop
Net current enclosed by the Amperian loop (A)
Number of turns per unit length (solenoid)
